results.possibleIdentity: Stanley J. Blanchard was an electronics engineer active in New York City during the late 1960s and 1970s. He resided at 4 Washington Square Village in Manhattan (Zip Code 10012), a location consistent with technical personnel associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Blanchard's technical expertise spanned electronic circuit design, computer-based pattern recognition, and medical electronics. He is most notably recognized as a co-inventor, alongside robotics pioneer George C. Devol and Paul S. Martin, of an "Automatic Object Adjustment" system (U.S. Patent 3,619,576, 1971), which utilized circular scanning and impulse patterns to recognize and orient objects. Additionally, he developed an electronic acupuncture apparatus (U.S. Patent 3,897,789, 1975) in consultation with master acupuncturist C.-Y. Ting. His work in the early 1970s focused on bridging traditional techniques with modern electrical devices to assist American physicians in therapeutic applications. Given his location and specialized skill set in circuit design and test equipment, he fits the profile of the engineers who registered with E.A.T. to provide technical services for artistic collaborations.
